briefing.opening: Good morning. Welcome to today's climate briefing, July 1, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Intensifying Global Climate Threats and the Demand for Urgent Action
sections.content: Globally, the severe impacts of climate change are fiercely striking in the form of prolonged high-temperature phenomena, with Europe recording its hottest June on record [37, 49, 54, 57, 60, 62, 65]. Such heatwaves are causing infrastructure damage, including buckling train tracks and crumbling roads, and raising concerns about worker fatalities due to extreme heat [4, 33]. Climate change is also emerging as a new threat to global supply chains, and its effects are being felt across the economy, with pension funds warning of financial risks due to climate change [1, 43]. Furthermore, a report has been released indicating that gas power plants for U.S. data centers will become a major source of climate change-related emissions, and significant ecological changes are being detected, such as the discovery of an endangered whale shark off the coast of Jeju [8, 34, 50]. Warnings from a Turkish cardiologist that climate change is now emerging as a health crisis, and from New Orleans residents about abandoning the city, underscore the urgent need for immediate action [53, 66].

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Expanding Domestic Efforts in Climate Change Adaptation and Carbon Neutrality
sections.content: Domestically, efforts towards climate change adaptation and carbon neutrality are actively expanding across local governments, businesses, and civil society. Yongin City achieved its second consecutive win in the national 'Excellent in Climate Crisis Adaptation' assessment, and Wonju City is expanding the distribution of new potato varieties adapted to climate change, highlighting leading adaptation efforts at the local government level [3, 5]. NHN has strengthened its disclosures on AI and climate change response through its 2025 Sustainability Report, and the Korea SMEs and Startups Agency (KOSME) has selected 30 companies for its carbon-neutral facility investment support program, encouraging industrial transition [2, 14, 16]. Gyeongsangbuk-do is emerging as a carbon-neutral industrial hub, having been successively selected for national core technology competitions for carbon reduction. Citizen-participatory programs and policy support are also active, such as Yeosu City's 'Carbon Moa Tantan-e' pilot operation and Suwon Special City's establishment of carbon accounting services [11, 12, 15, 17]. Furthermore, awareness and action are increasing across society, including the cement industry's response to carbon neutrality challenges, Yeongsanpo Girls' Middle School's operation of an environmental education week, and the Presbyterian Church in Korea (PCK) Jeonnam-Gwangju Men's Association's presentation of the church's role [9, 18, 19, 20, 21, 22, 23, 64].

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating Large-Scale Renewable Energy Investment and Clean Energy Transition
sections.content: The acceleration of the clean energy transition is significantly emerging as a core driver in responding to climate change. Notably, SK Group, in partnership with global private equity firm KKR, has launched Korea's largest 10GW-class renewable energy company and announced plans to expand its generation capacity to 10GW by 2031 [25, 26, 27, 28, 29, 30, 31, 32]. This is expected to bring significant changes to the domestic renewable energy market. Concurrently, the Korea Racing Authority (KRA) is also participating in carbon neutrality efforts by holding a Carbon Neutrality Promotion Committee to review its renewable energy expansion plans [10]. Internationally, clean energy policy cooperation is strengthening, with Virginia officially rejoining the Regional Greenhouse Gas Initiative (RGGI) [61]. In Switzerland, discussions on future energy scenarios are underway, exploring conditions for the coexistence of nuclear and renewable energy, thus seeking harmonious utilization strategies for various energy sources [24].

sections.title: 🌍 International Climate Discourse and Complex Challenges
sections.content: In the international community, the discourse on climate change is becoming increasingly complex and multi-layered. Amid escalating blame games between nations, such as Paris criticizing the U.S. over climate change, the U.S. Democratic Party has begun to re-prioritize climate change as a major agenda item [35, 36]. Concurrently, a Rice University study pointed out that climate change is missing from the narratives shaping modern culture, and the climate agenda is expanding across various sectors, with athletes envisioning the future of sports amidst climate change and the Anglican Church discussing the church's response [38, 39, 40, 41]. The importance of inclusive climate action is being emphasized, with methods proposed to make climate action more inclusive for 1.3 billion people with disabilities, and a climate action plan launched for the Mediterranean region [44, 45, 46]. While some experts offer a positive outlook, suggesting that humanity has faced difficult situations before and can navigate its way through [51, 55], conflicting policy movements and debates persist, as evidenced by numerous editorials in the British press supporting North Sea drilling [48, 63, 67, 68].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing highlights the severe impacts of climate change, alongside the simultaneous and multifaceted domestic and international efforts to overcome them. This is a critical juncture where accelerated clean energy transition and comprehensive climate action, coupled with sustained vigilance, are more urgent than ever. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.description: Chief scientist says dangerous heatwaves, which are getting more likely, ‘bring home the implications of climate change’The month of June was the hottest in England on record, driven by a searing heatwave in the final days of the month, which for the first time had red heat alerts for three days, according to Met Office data.The Met Office said provisional statistics showed Wales and the UK as a whole had recorded their second-warmest June since 1884.
